full_text: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Leadership Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview The Chief Information Security Officer is the executive responsible for an organization's information security strategy, risk management, compliance, and incident response program. As cyberattacks grow more sophisticated and frequent, and as regulatory requirements multiply globally (GDPR, NIS2, DORA, SEC cybersecurity disclosure rules), the CISO role has elevated from a technical specialist to a board-level strategic leader. CISOs at major organizations now report directly to the CEO or board and are central to every major technology and business decision. What the Role Involves Responsibilities include setting and executing the enterprise security strategy, managing security budgets, building and leading security teams, overseeing regulatory compliance programs, communicating cyber risk to the board and C-suite, managing cyber insurance, leading the response to major security incidents, and building the security culture across the organization. Modern CISOs also oversee AI security governance—managing risks from AI-powered threats and from the organization's own AI deployments. They engage with peer CISOs, government agencies, and industry groups on collective threat intelligence sharing. Key Skills Required Broad technical security expertise combined with business acumen, executive communication, financial management, and leadership skills define the CISO. Certifications (CISSP, CISM, CRISC) and a track record of building security programs are standard credentials. The ability to quantify cyber risk in business terms (financial impact models), build board-level relationships, and lead organizational change are the qualities that separate exceptional CISOs from competent ones. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Exceptional CISOs are both technically credible and politically astute. They communicate risk without creating panic, influence without direct authority over business units, and maintain calm under the pressure of a major breach. They are life-long learners who stay ahead of evolving threats while running large, complex organizations. Integrity and trustworthiness are foundational—the CISO is the guardian of organizational trust. Career Progression Security Engineer → Security Manager → VP/Director of Security → CISO. CISOs at major corporations often have 15–25 years of experience. Many work through consulting or advisory roles before taking on CISO positions. Board roles and advisory positions often follow senior CISO tenures. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $180,000–$240,000 (mid-market company CISO), $250,000–$400,000 (large enterprise CISO), $400,000–$700,000+ (Fortune 500 CISO with total compensation including equity). Annual salary growth of 9–13% is projected, driven by regulatory pressure and the increasing strategic value of cyber risk management. full_text: Solutions Architect IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical / Advisory Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Solutions Architects design technical solutions to specific customer or business problems, translating requirements into implementable architectures using a combination of technology components, platforms, and custom development. The role exists both within technology companies—as customer-facing technical advisors (pre-sales and post-sales)—and within enterprises, where internal Solutions Architects design cross-system solutions for complex business challenges. As technology stacks grow more complex and vendor landscapes more crowded, skilled Solutions Architects who can cut through complexity to pragmatic, well-designed solutions are in consistently high demand. What the Role Involves Work includes conducting discovery sessions with stakeholders to understand business and technical requirements, designing end-to-end technical architectures, producing architecture documents and diagrams, evaluating and recommending technology platforms and vendors, creating proof-of-concept implementations, presenting solutions to technical and non-technical audiences, and guiding implementation teams during delivery. Customer-facing Solutions Architects also develop SOWs (Statements of Work), manage technical aspects of RFP responses, and act as trusted technical advisors throughout the customer lifecycle. Key Skills Required Broad technical depth across at least one major domain (cloud, data, security, networking, AI) combined with excellent communication and presentation skills. The ability to whiteboard complex architectures, create clear technical documentation, and translate business requirements into technical designs is core. Customer-facing roles require consultative selling skills, executive presence, and the ability to build trust rapidly. Relevant certifications (AWS, GCP, Azure, Salesforce) and vertical industry knowledge add significant value. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Solutions Architects are natural connectors—they enjoy the intellectual puzzle of fitting the right technology to a specific problem. They are excellent listeners who ask the right questions before jumping to solutions. They are comfortable presenting to both engineers and C-suite executives and can adjust their communication register accordingly. They are energized by variety, as each engagement brings different technical challenges and business contexts. Career Progression Senior Engineer → Solutions Architect → Senior Solutions Architect → Principal Solutions Architect → Distinguished Architect or Director of Solutions Architecture. Customer-facing roles at cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) are particularly prestigious and well-compensated. Annual salary growth of 7–10% is projected, consistent with enterprise technology investment growth. full_text: Data Governance Manager IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Management & Strategy Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Data Governance Managers establish and oversee the policies, standards, processes, and organizational structures that ensure data is accurate, available, consistent, secure, and used in compliance with regulations. The role has evolved from a largely bureaucratic compliance function to a strategic enabler of data-driven and AI-driven business. What the Role Involves Core responsibilities include designing and implementing data governance frameworks (policies, standards, data quality rules), managing data catalogs and business glossaries (Collibra, Alation, Microsoft Purview), establishing data stewardship networks across business units, overseeing data lineage and provenance tracking, managing regulatory data compliance programs (GDPR data mapping, data subject rights), defining data classification and handling policies, and measuring data quality metrics. Data Governance Managers increasingly oversee AI data governance—ensuring that training data for AI systems meets quality, fairness, and compliance requirements. Key Skills Required Knowledge of data management frameworks (DAMA-DMBOK), privacy regulations, data catalog tooling, SQL and basic data analysis, and enterprise data architecture is foundational. Experience with Collibra, Informatica, or similar data governance platforms, combined with change management skills for driving cultural adoption, is expected. Soft skills—particularly the ability to influence business stakeholders without direct authority and to make governance frameworks feel enabling rather than restrictive—are the defining competencies. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Effective Data Governance Managers combine the precision of a data professional with the organizational skills of a program manager. They are persistent in building data quality culture in organizations that have historically neglected it. They communicate the business value of governance clearly—connecting data quality to AI effectiveness, regulatory risk, and business decision quality—rather than advocating governance for its own sake. Career Progression Data Analyst / Business Analyst → Data Governance Analyst → Data Governance Manager → Director of Data Governance → VP/Head of Data Governance or Chief Data Officer (CDO). full_text: Engineering Manager IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Leadership Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Engineering Managers lead software engineering teams, balancing technical direction, people development, project delivery, and cross-functional collaboration. As organizations scale their engineering capabilities and navigate the complexity of AI-assisted development, effective Engineering Managers become a critical bottleneck—or accelerant—for engineering performance. The transition from individual contributor to manager is one of the most significant and challenging career pivots in tech, but those who navigate it well are handsomely rewarded. What the Role Involves Day-to-day responsibilities include conducting regular 1:1s with direct reports, setting technical direction for the team, participating in system design reviews, managing hiring and interview processes, owning team delivery commitments, escalating and unblocking impediments, managing performance reviews and compensation decisions, fostering psychological safety and a culture of learning, and coordinating with product managers and other engineering teams. Engineering Managers also communicate upward—providing status, surfacing risks, and representing their team's interests in leadership forums. Key Skills Required A solid technical foundation (usually 5+ years of software engineering experience) combined with people leadership skills is the baseline. Key competencies include active listening, coaching and mentorship, difficult feedback delivery, conflict resolution, and the ability to drive performance without micromanagement. Technical credibility—enough to evaluate code quality, architecture decisions, and technical hiring candidates—is important but should not dominate at the expense of leadership focus. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed The best Engineering Managers are primarily motivated by the success of others—they measure their own impact through their team's outcomes, not personal technical contributions. They are excellent listeners who create the conditions for their team to do their best work. They are comfortable with ambiguity and able to prioritize ruthlessly. They invest in their own management skill development as diligently as they once invested in technical skills. Career Progression Senior Engineer → Engineering Manager → Senior Engineering Manager → Director of Engineering → VP of Engineering → SVP/CTO. Span of control (number of reports, team count) typically increases with each level. Many EMs return to the IC track if they find management is not fulfilling. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $140,000–$175,000 (EM), $180,000–$230,000 (senior EM/director), $235,000–$400,000+ (VP with equity). Annual salary growth of 7–10% is projected, reflecting sustained demand as engineering organizations expand. full_text: Scrum Master / Agile Coach IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Process & Culture Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Scrum Masters and Agile Coaches facilitate agile software development processes, helping teams and organizations adopt agile principles to deliver software more effectively. While the Scrum Master role is team-level—facilitating sprint ceremonies, removing impediments, and coaching the team on Scrum—the Agile Coach operates at an organizational level, transforming how entire departments or enterprises develop software. As distributed teams, complex multi-team programs, and AI-augmented development change how software is built, skilled facilitators of agile ways of working remain in demand. What the Role Involves For Scrum Masters: facilitating daily standups, sprint planning, retrospectives, and reviews; tracking sprint metrics (velocity, burndown); removing team impediments; coaching the team on self-organization and cross-functionality; shielding the team from external distractions. For Agile Coaches: designing and facilitating organizational agile transformations, implementing frameworks like SAFe, LeSS, or Disciplined Agile, coaching leadership on agile mindset, measuring and improving team performance metrics (lead time, cycle time, deployment frequency), and building internal agile coaching capability. Key Skills Required Scrum certification (PSM, CSM) is typically expected for Scrum Masters; ICAgile (ICP-ACC), CEC (Certified Enterprise Coach), or SAFe credentials for Agile Coaches. Facilitation skills, conflict resolution, coaching and mentoring, and the ability to influence without authority are the core competencies. For Agile Coaches, organizational change management and business strategy literacy are important. Soft skills—patience, empathy, and the ability to hold space for a team's struggle without prescribing solutions—define excellence. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed The best Scrum Masters and Agile Coaches are servant leaders who find satisfaction in enabling others rather than being the center of attention. They are psychologically secure—not threatened by team success that doesn't visibly reflect their contribution. They are curious, lifelong learners who experiment with new facilitation techniques and adjust their coaching approach to each team's unique context. They are also courageous in naming what is not working and holding teams accountable to their commitments. Career Progression Junior Scrum Master → Scrum Master → Senior Scrum Master → Agile Coach → Senior Agile Coach → Enterprise Agile Coach → Head of Agile / VP of Engineering Effectiveness. Many transition into Engineering Management, Product Management, or organizational effectiveness consulting. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $80,000–$110,000 (Scrum Master), $115,000–$155,000 (senior SM / junior Agile Coach), $160,000–$220,000 (senior/enterprise Agile Coach). TPgMs increasingly manage AI/ML infrastructure programs, cloud migration programs, and large-scale platform modernization initiatives that require deep technical understanding alongside program management rigor. Key Skills Required PMP or equivalent project management certification, combined with 5+ years of software engineering experience, is the typical baseline. Proficiency in project tracking tools (Jira, Asana, Linear, Confluence), risk management, stakeholder management, executive communication, and the ability to quickly understand technical designs across diverse domains (networking, ML, distributed systems) are core competencies. Soft skills: political savvy in navigating large organizations, ability to drive decisions from diverse stakeholders with competing interests. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Excellent TPgMs are highly organized but thrive in ambiguity—when a program's scope is fluid and dependencies are unclear, they create the structure that enables teams to move forward. They are natural pattern-recognizers who see risks before they become incidents and have the communication skills to escalate them constructively. They are trusted equally by engineering teams (who respect their technical literacy) and business leaders (who value their clear executive communication). The IC track extends to Distinguished TPgM at major tech companies. Increasingly, UX Designers are involved in AI feature design—creating interactions for chatbots, recommendation systems, and AI-generated content. Key Skills Required Proficiency in Figma (the industry-standard design tool), competency in conducting user research methodologies, understanding of design systems and component libraries, basic HTML/CSS literacy for cross-functional collaboration, and accessibility standards (WCAG 2.1/2.2) are foundational. Knowledge of motion design, conversational UI design, and design analytics tools (Hotjar, FullStory) distinguishes senior designers. Soft skills: exceptional communication and the ability to defend design decisions with evidence while remaining open to iteration. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Great UX Designers have deep empathy for users and an almost missionary zeal for removing friction from human-computer interaction. They are equally comfortable in the messy qualitative world of user research and the precise quantitative world of A/B testing. They advocate for users in product discussions and are comfortable doing so even when user needs conflict with business preferences. They maintain high aesthetic standards while pragmatically balancing delivery constraints. Career Progression Junior UX Designer → UX Designer → Senior UX Designer → Lead/Principal UX Designer → UX Director → VP of Design / Chief Design Officer. As digital systems underpin every business function and the regulatory scrutiny of technology risk intensifies (EU DORA, Basel IV for financial services, AI Act), organizations need senior professionals who can translate technical risk into business terms and build robust risk management frameworks. What the Role Involves Responsibilities include conducting technology risk assessments using frameworks such as NIST RMF, COBIT, or FAIR, designing risk control frameworks and key risk indicators (KRIs), managing third-party and vendor risk programs, overseeing technology continuity and disaster recovery planning, ensuring regulatory compliance (DORA, SOX IT controls, PCI DSS, ISO 27001), managing AI model risk governance, and reporting to board risk committees. Technology Risk Managers also work on risk quantification—using models like FAIR to express technology risk in financial terms that boards can act on. Key Skills Required Expertise in risk management frameworks (NIST RMF, FAIR, COBIT), regulatory compliance, and IT audit fundamentals (CISA certification is common), combined with enough technical literacy to assess cybersecurity, cloud, and AI risks effectively. Financial modeling for risk quantification and the ability to present risk in business terms to non-technical boards are essential senior competencies. Soft skills: intellectual rigor, objectivity, and the courage to escalate uncomfortable risk findings to leadership. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed The best Technology Risk Managers combine the analytical precision of an auditor with the business acumen of a strategist. They are comfortable communicating with both technical teams and board members and can translate between these audiences fluently. They maintain independence and objectivity even under organizational pressure to downplay risks, and they are constructive partners rather than blockers—helping the business understand and manage risk rather than simply saying no. Career Progression IT Auditor / Risk Analyst → Technology Risk Manager → Senior Risk Manager → Head of Technology Risk → Chief Risk Officer (CRO) or VP of Risk. Financial services firms, where technology risk is most formalized, offer particularly well-defined progression paths. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $100,000–$135,000 (manager), $140,000–$185,000 (senior/director), $190,000–$320,000+ (head/CRO with bonuses in financial services). Annual salary growth of 7–11% is projected, driven by increasing regulatory requirements and AI risk governance demands. full_text: IT Business Analyst IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Analysis & Consulting Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview IT Business Analysts bridge the gap between business stakeholders and technology teams, translating business needs into clear technical requirements that enable effective software development and digital transformation. The role is foundational to every large-scale technology program and enterprise system implementation. As AI and automation change how IT solutions are built and deployed, business analysts who understand both the evolving technical landscape and the human dimensions of change will remain indispensable. What the Role Involves Core activities include eliciting and documenting business and functional requirements (user stories, use cases, process flows), facilitating workshops and interviews with stakeholders, creating and managing product backlogs, performing gap analyses between current and future state processes, mapping data requirements for system integrations, participating in user acceptance testing (UAT), managing scope change processes, and creating business cases for technology investments. Senior BAs also perform process redesign, develop feasibility analyses, and advise on technology selection. Key Skills Required Requirements elicitation and documentation, process modeling (BPMN, UML), stakeholder management, facilitation, analytical thinking, and proficiency in collaboration tools (Confluence, Jira, Miro) are foundational. Familiarity with agile development methodologies (SAFe, Scrum, Kanban) and increasing comfort with data analysis tools (SQL, Tableau, Power BI) distinguishes senior practitioners. Certifications such as CBAP (Certified Business Analysis Professional) or PMI-PBA are recognized credentials. Soft skills: active listening, diplomatic communication, and the ability to resolve conflicting stakeholder requirements. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Excellent Business Analysts are curious about how things work—how business processes create value, how data flows through systems, how technology can improve operational efficiency. They are patient, thorough documentarians who ensure nothing falls through the cracks between business intent and technical implementation. They are naturally diplomatic, able to facilitate agreement between stakeholders with competing priorities without losing the thread of the project goal. Career Progression Junior BA → Business Analyst → Senior BA → Lead BA / BA Manager → Product Owner or Product Manager → Director of Business Analysis or Head of Digital Programs. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $70,000–$95,000 (junior-mid), $100,000–$135,000 (senior), $140,000–$185,000 (lead/director). Annual salary growth of 5–8% is projected, consistent with sustained enterprise technology program demand. full_text: Chief AI Officer (CAIO) IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Leadership Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview The Chief AI Officer is an emerging C-suite role responsible for developing and executing an organization's AI strategy, governing AI use responsibly, building AI capabilities, and driving enterprise-wide AI adoption. As generative AI transforms how every function operates—from marketing and supply chain to finance and HR—organizations need a dedicated executive to coordinate AI initiatives, avoid duplication, manage AI-related risks, and ensure the company captures AI's competitive potential. The U.S. government mandated CAIO roles across federal agencies in 2024, accelerating adoption across both public and private sectors. What the Role Involves The CAIO develops the organization's AI vision and multi-year roadmap, builds the AI center of excellence and talent strategy, establishes AI governance frameworks (use-case review, ethics review, risk controls), oversees AI budget allocation, communicates AI strategy to the board and external stakeholders, manages relationships with AI platform vendors (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Microsoft), and measures enterprise AI ROI. The role also involves workforce transformation—building AI literacy across the organization and managing the change associated with AI-driven automation. Key Skills Required Deep understanding of AI/ML capabilities and limitations, strong business strategy acumen, executive leadership, communication and influencing skills, and knowledge of AI governance and ethics frameworks are the defining competencies. A technical background in AI/ML or data science combined with P&L management experience is the ideal profile. Change management expertise and the ability to inspire AI confidence in skeptical organizations are critical soft skills. Emerging certifications and executive education programs in AI strategy (MIT, INSEAD, Kellogg) are increasingly common credentials. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Effective CAIOs are visionaries who can see the transformative potential of AI across the business while being pragmatic enough to sequence adoption in ways that actually succeed. They inspire both excitement and confidence—they know enough about AI to be credible when something works but also when it doesn't. They are collaborative executives who work across all business functions and balance the urgency to move fast with the responsibility to govern AI responsibly. Career Progression This role is typically reached from Head of AI, VP of AI, CDO, CTO, or occasionally CISO backgrounds. Many first-time CAIOs are internal candidates who have led significant AI programs. The role may evolve over the decade as AI becomes embedded infrastructure rather than a distinct initiative. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $200,000–$280,000 (director/VP-equivalent at mid-market companies), $300,000–$600,000+ (CAIO at large enterprises with total compensation including bonuses and equity). Annual salary growth of 15–20% is projected near-term, making this among the fastest-appreciating executive roles through 2027. full_text: Technical Writer / Documentation Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Communication & Content Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Technical Writers and Documentation Engineers create, organize, and maintain the technical documentation, tutorials, API references, and knowledge bases that enable developers, operators, and end users to effectively use software products. Despite the rise of AI-assisted documentation generation, the strategic design of documentation—understanding what users need to know, in what order, and at what level of detail—remains a distinctly human skill. Developer experience has become a competitive differentiator, and documentation quality is central to it. What the Role Involves Work includes writing and maintaining API references, developer guides, conceptual overviews, quick-start tutorials, integration guides, and release notes. Technical Writers collaborate closely with engineers and product managers to understand new features, participate in product reviews to flag documentation gaps, manage documentation platforms (Docusaurus, Readme, Confluence), and apply information architecture principles to organize large content sets. Senior practitioners lead documentation strategy, establish style guides, and implement docs-as-code workflows (writing documentation in Markdown, stored in Git, reviewed via pull requests). Key Skills Required Excellent technical writing craft—clarity, concision, appropriate structure—combined with sufficient technical literacy to understand and accurately explain complex software systems. Proficiency in Markdown, Git, static site generators (MkDocs, Docusaurus), API documentation tools (Swagger/OpenAPI), and diagramming (Mermaid, draw.io) is expected. Understanding of developer experience (DX) principles and content strategy distinguishes senior practitioners. Soft skills: the rare combination of precision with language and a genuine desire to help readers succeed. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed The best Technical Writers combine the linguistic precision of an editor with the systematic thinking of an engineer. They are instinctive teachers who put themselves in the reader's shoes and are never satisfied with documentation that technically contains the right information but buries it. They are curious about technology and not afraid to ask engineers naive questions to ensure they truly understand what they're documenting. Career Progression Junior Technical Writer → Technical Writer → Senior Technical Writer → Principal Technical Writer or Documentation Engineering Manager → Head of Developer Documentation → VP of Developer Experience. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $65,000–$90,000 (junior-mid), $95,000–$135,000 (senior), $140,000–$200,000 (principal/lead at top tech companies). Annual salary growth of 5–8% is projected, with documentation engineering roles at AI and developer platform companies commanding a premium. full_text: API Architect IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview API Architects design the interfaces through which software systems communicate—defining the standards, patterns, governance, and lifecycle processes for APIs across an organization or product ecosystem. In an API-first world where organizations monetize data and services through developer ecosystems and where internal microservices communicate primarily via APIs, the quality, consistency, and reliability of APIs directly impacts developer productivity, partner integrations, and business outcomes. The role has gained formal recognition as companies mature their API programs. What the Role Involves Work includes designing RESTful, GraphQL, gRPC, and event-driven (AsyncAPI) interfaces following OpenAPI standards, establishing API design guidelines and review processes, managing API versioning and deprecation strategies, designing API security architectures (OAuth 2.0, API gateways, rate limiting), leading API platform selection and governance (Kong, Apigee, AWS API Gateway), mentoring teams on API design patterns, and supporting the monetization of APIs as products. API Architects increasingly work on AI API design—defining how LLM capabilities are exposed as APIs to internal and external consumers. Key Skills Required Deep knowledge of API design patterns, OpenAPI/Swagger, authentication protocols (OAuth 2.0, OIDC), API gateway technologies, and service mesh architecture is foundational. Experience with API management platforms, microservices architecture, and developer experience design is expected at senior levels. Soft skills: the ability to write clear, opinionated design guidelines and to socialize them effectively across engineering teams with varying levels of API design maturity. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed API Architects have strong opinions about design—they are unsatisfied by inconsistent or poorly designed APIs and are motivated to establish coherent design systems across large, distributed engineering organizations. They are excellent technical communicators who can write guidelines that engineers actually read and follow. They balance the ideal design with pragmatic delivery constraints and understand that an API, once published, is a contract that must be honored. Career Progression Backend Engineer → Senior Engineer → API Architect → Principal/Distinguished Architect → Head of API Platform → VP of Engineering (Platform). Many transition into Solutions Architecture or Developer Advocacy roles. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $120,000–$155,000 (architect), $160,000–$210,000 (senior/principal), $215,000–$300,000+ (head/distinguished at API-platform companies). Annual salary growth of 7–10% is projected, consistent with the expansion of the API economy. Working either in consulting firms (Accenture, Deloitte, McKinsey, Big 4) or as independent advisors, technology consultants bring an outside perspective and broad cross-industry experience that internal teams often lack. The accelerating pace of AI adoption, cloud migration, and digital transformation has created significant demand for advisors who can help organizations navigate complexity effectively. What the Role Involves Core activities include conducting technology assessments and maturity evaluations, developing digital and AI strategies, leading technology vendor selection processes, designing operating model transformations, managing large-scale technology program delivery (often alongside major system integrations), building business cases for technology investment, training client teams, and transferring knowledge. Senior consultants develop thought leadership, contribute to practice development, and manage client relationships. Consulting life involves significant travel, varied industry exposure, and project-based intense cycles. Key Skills Required A combination of technical expertise (depth in one or more domains—cloud, AI, cybersecurity, ERP, data engineering), business strategy skills, analytical rigor, structured communication (PowerPoint, executive briefing), and project management capability is the consulting toolkit. Soft skills—client relationship management, the ability to manage ambiguity in new client environments, team leadership, and the gravitas to challenge senior client executives constructively—define progression in consulting. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Successful Technology Consultants are intellectually versatile—able to rapidly understand new industries, technologies, and organizational contexts. They are energized by variety and the challenge of delivering high-quality work under tight deadlines. They are naturally curious, asking questions that clients have not thought to ask, and are comfortable delivering recommendations that challenge the client's existing thinking. Career Progression Analyst → Consultant → Senior Consultant → Manager → Senior Manager → Director → Principal/Partner. Consulting firm timelines are typically 2–3 years per level. Many technology consultants exit to industry roles (strategy, head of transformation, CDO) after 5–10 years. Annual salary growth of 7–10% is projected, with AI strategy consulting commands at a significant premium. full_text: Game Developer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Game Developers design and build interactive entertainment experiences across mobile, PC, console, and emerging XR platforms. Beyond entertainment, game development skills are increasingly applied to gamification of enterprise applications, serious games for training and simulation, virtual worlds, and interactive experiences for the metaverse. The global games market exceeds $200 billion annually and is growing, driven by mobile gaming, cloud gaming, and the expanding cultural and economic significance of interactive media. What the Role Involves Core activities include game design (mechanics, systems, narrative), programming (gameplay logic, AI, physics, rendering), game engine development (Unity, Unreal Engine, Godot), tools and pipeline development, optimization for performance across hardware targets, integration of online services (matchmaking, leaderboards, in-app purchases), live operations (keeping live games healthy post-launch), and building game AI systems. Growing areas include AI-generated game content, procedural generation enhanced by LLMs, and the development of immersive XR game experiences. Key Skills Required C++ (Unreal Engine) or C# (Unity), strong mathematics (linear algebra, physics, trigonometry), rendering pipeline knowledge (shaders, rendering APIs—Vulkan, DirectX, Metal), and game engine architecture understanding are foundational. Specializations in AI, networking, tools engineering, or rendering attract distinct skill requirements. Soft skills: the ability to build collaborative, creative team environments where designers, artists, and programmers work iteratively toward a shared creative vision. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Game Developers are typically passionate players themselves, with an intrinsic motivation to create experiences that delight and challenge. They have high aesthetic standards and a "player-first" perspective. They are creative problem-solvers who enjoy the intersection of artistry and engineering. They are resilient—game development involves significant iteration, scope changes, and occasional "crunch," requiring adaptability and perseverance. Career Progression Junior Game Developer → Game Developer → Senior Developer → Lead Developer → Technical Director → CTO or Studio Director. Independent/indie paths are common, as are transitions into simulation engineering, film VFX, or XR application development. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $65,000–$90,000 (junior), $95,000–$140,000 (senior), $145,000–$210,000+ (lead/tech director at major studios). AAA studio salaries have increased significantly due to talent competition from tech companies. Annual salary growth of 5–9% is projected. full_text: Embedded Systems Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Embedded Systems Engineers develop the software that runs directly on specialized hardware—microcontrollers, FPGAs, ASICs, and purpose-built processors—in devices such as consumer electronics, medical devices, industrial controllers, automotive ECUs, aerospace systems, and IoT sensors. As billions of new edge devices are deployed and as AI inference increasingly happens on-device, embedded systems expertise becomes more critical than ever. The specialization is perennially undersupplied relative to demand. What the Role Involves Core responsibilities include writing firmware in C/C++ (and increasingly Rust) for bare-metal or RTOS environments (FreeRTOS, Zephyr, ThreadX), developing device drivers, managing hardware interfaces (SPI, I2C, UART, CAN, Ethernet), optimizing code for resource-constrained processors (limited RAM, flash, and CPU cycles), integrating sensors and actuators, writing bootloaders and OTA update systems, debugging with oscilloscopes and logic analyzers, and meeting real-time and safety requirements. A growing area is deploying tinyML models on microcontrollers using frameworks like TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers and Edge Impulse. Key Skills Required Deep C/C++ proficiency, hardware architecture knowledge (ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, x86 for embedded), real-time operating systems (RTOS), peripheral interfaces, and debugging with hardware tools (JTAG, oscilloscope, logic analyzer) are foundational. Knowledge of functional safety standards (IEC 61508, ISO 26262, DO-178C) is essential for safety-critical domains. Rust for embedded is a growing and valued skill. Soft skills: meticulous attention to detail—firmware bugs in deployed hardware cannot be patched as easily as web software bugs. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Embedded Engineers are often deeply interested in the physical interaction of software with hardware—they find the mapping between code instructions and physical voltage changes intrinsically fascinating. They are methodical debuggers with patience for root-cause analysis in environments where traditional debugging tools don't work. They have high standards for code reliability and are uncomfortable shipping anything that is not exhaustively tested. Career Progression Embedded Engineer → Senior Embedded Engineer → Principal Embedded Engineer or Embedded Architect → Head of Firmware → VP of Engineering (Hardware Products). Specializations in automotive, medical, aerospace, or consumer electronics create distinct company ecosystems. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $95,000–$125,000 (mid-level), $130,000–$175,000 (senior), $180,000–$260,000 (principal/architect). Annual salary growth of 7–10% is projected, driven by IoT, automotive, and edge AI investment. full_text: Product Manager (General Technology) IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Product & Strategy Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Product Managers define what technology products are built, for whom, and why. They own the product vision, strategy, and roadmap, and are accountable for the product's business outcomes. Often described as the "CEO of the product," PMs make the hard prioritization decisions that determine where engineering capacity is invested. As software eating the world accelerates and as every company becomes a technology company to some degree, skilled Product Managers who can synthesize user needs, business strategy, and technical possibilities are among the most sought-after professionals in the industry. What the Role Involves Core activities include market and user research, defining product strategy and OKRs, managing the product backlog, writing product requirements (PRDs, user stories), prioritizing features, leading cross-functional product squads (designers, engineers, data analysts), communicating product strategy to leadership, defining success metrics and tracking them post-launch, managing the product's commercial performance, and representing the voice of the customer in engineering decisions. Modern PMs also manage AI feature development increasingly, requiring ML literacy. Key Skills Required User research methodologies, data analysis (SQL, Tableau, product analytics tools like Amplitude or Mixpanel), stakeholder management, prioritization frameworks (RICE, ICE, MoSCoW), writing clear PRDs, and product strategy are foundational. Domain expertise in the product's market, combined with enough technical literacy to converse credibly with engineers, is a critical combination. Soft skills—leadership without authority, storytelling, and the ability to say "no" diplomatically—define excellent PMs. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Outstanding Product Managers are driven by user impact and are endlessly curious about the problems they are solving. They have strong opinions but hold them loosely, updating them rapidly when faced with new data. They are natural collaborators who build trust across engineering, design, and business functions. They are comfortable making decisions with incomplete information and communicating those decisions with clarity and conviction. Career Progression Associate PM → PM → Senior PM → Principal PM / Group PM → Director of Product → VP of Product → CPO. PM tracks at major tech companies (Google APM, Facebook RPM, Microsoft MSPMP) are highly formalized. Many CPOs and founders began in general PM roles. full_text: Kubernetes / Container Platform Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Kubernetes and Container Platform Engineers build, operate, and optimize the container orchestration infrastructure on which modern cloud-native applications run. As Kubernetes has become the de facto substrate for deploying software at scale, deep expertise in cluster management, networking, storage, and the Kubernetes ecosystem has become one of the most in-demand and durable cloud infrastructure skill sets. The growing complexity of multi-cluster, multi-cloud Kubernetes deployments and the need to run AI workloads on GPU-enabled Kubernetes clusters will sustain demand for this specialization through 2035. What the Role Involves Responsibilities include provisioning and managing Kubernetes clusters (EKS, GKE, AKS, on-premises), designing cluster networking (CNI plugins, service meshes), implementing storage solutions (CSI drivers, persistent volumes), managing workload scheduling and resource optimization (HPA, VPA, KEDA, cluster autoscaler), implementing GitOps workflows (Argo CD, Flux), managing RBAC and security policies (OPA/Gatekeeper, Pod Security Standards), ensuring cluster reliability and observability, and managing GPU nodes for AI training and inference workloads. Key Skills Required Expert-level Kubernetes knowledge is required, including CKA/CKAD/CKS certifications. Experience with Helm, Kustomize, Argo CD, service mesh (Istio, Cilium), observability stacks, Terraform or Crossplane for cluster provisioning, and at least one cloud provider's managed Kubernetes service is expected. GPU infrastructure management (NVIDIA device plugins, MIG partitioning) is a growing specialty. Soft skills: the ability to build self-service platform tooling that simplifies Kubernetes complexity for application developers. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Container Platform Engineers are systems thinkers who find the scheduling, networking, and storage challenges of distributed systems genuinely exciting. They are meticulous operators who care deeply about cluster reliability and have a low tolerance for flaky infrastructure. They are also excellent teachers—because the platform exists to serve application developers, helping those developers understand and use Kubernetes effectively is part of the job. Career Progression DevOps Engineer → Platform Engineer → Senior Platform/Kubernetes Engineer → Staff Platform Engineer → Principal Platform Architect → Head of Platform Engineering. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $110,000–$145,000 (mid-level), $150,000–$200,000 (senior), $205,000–$280,000 (staff/principal). Annual salary growth of 8–12% is projected, driven by cloud-native infrastructure expansion and AI workload deployment on Kubernetes. full_text: Database Administrator / Data Platform Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Database Administrators (DBAs) and Data Platform Engineers design, implement, optimize, and maintain the database systems and data storage platforms that underpin every application and business intelligence system. While traditional DBA skills (tuning queries, managing backups, ensuring availability) remain relevant, the role has evolved toward data platform engineering—managing cloud-native databases, data warehouses, and lakehouses at scale, and increasingly supporting AI workloads that require high-performance vector databases and real-time feature stores. What the Role Involves Work includes database design and schema management, performance tuning and query optimization, implementing backup, recovery, and high availability strategies (replication, failover clusters), managing cloud databases (RDS, Cloud Spanner, Cosmos DB, AlloyDB), designing and optimizing data warehouses (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift), implementing database security (encryption, access controls, auditing), managing database change deployments via CI/CD, and increasingly, managing vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, pgvector) for AI search and retrieval applications. Key Skills Required Deep expertise in SQL and one or more database systems (PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQL Server, Oracle), knowledge of cloud database services, performance tuning skills (execution plans, indexing strategies, partitioning), backup and recovery procedures, and scripting (Python, Bash) are core. Experience with data warehousing platforms, NoSQL databases (MongoDB, DynamoDB, Cassandra), and modern data lakehouse architectures (Delta Lake, Apache Iceberg) distinguishes senior practitioners. Soft skills: the ability to collaborate with application developers to design schemas that balance normalization with performance. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Outstanding DBAs and Data Platform Engineers have an almost obsessive attention to data integrity—they are disturbed by the possibility of data loss or corruption in a way that motivates meticulous backup testing and failover validation. They are systematic performance investigators who enjoy the detective work of understanding why a query is slow. They are also pragmatic architects who know when to choose simplicity over theoretical perfection. Career Progression Junior DBA → DBA → Senior DBA → Database Architect or Data Platform Engineer → Principal Data Platform Engineer → Head of Data Infrastructure. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $85,000–$115,000 (mid-level), $120,000–$160,000 (senior), $165,000–$230,000 (architect/principal). Annual salary growth of 6–9% is projected, with vector database and AI infrastructure expertise commanding a premium. full_text: Chief Technology Officer (CTO) IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Leadership Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview The Chief Technology Officer is the executive responsible for the company's technological direction—determining the technology strategy, leading engineering and product development, managing the R&D agenda, and ensuring that technology capabilities give the company a competitive advantage. At technology-centric companies, the CTO is often the most influential executive after the CEO. As AI fundamentally reshapes what is technically possible and as technology differentiation becomes the primary axis of competition across industries, the CTO role has never carried greater strategic weight. What the Role Involves Responsibilities include setting the technology vision and multi-year strategy, leading engineering and product organizations (at some companies the CPO reports to the CTO), overseeing architecture decisions, managing the technology budget, driving the company's AI and innovation agenda, recruiting and developing technical leadership, managing technology vendor relationships, communicating the technology story to investors, customers, and the board, and ensuring technology systems meet reliability, security, and scalability requirements. CTOs also represent the company externally—speaking at conferences, contributing to policy discussions, and building the company's technical reputation. Key Skills Required Deep technical credibility (typically 15–20+ years of software engineering and technical leadership experience), business strategy skills, executive communication, financial management, team building, and the ability to hire and inspire world-class engineers are the defining competencies. Broad technology literacy across AI, cloud, security, and data is expected. Soft skills—vision setting, organizational change leadership, and the executive presence to inspire engineering organizations toward ambitious goals—define excellence at this level. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Exceptional CTOs combine technical depth with organizational leadership and business acumen. They are talent magnets—engineers want to work for them because they set ambitious technical visions, protect engineering culture, and develop people. They are strategic thinkers who see technology not as an end but as a means to competitive advantage, and they communicate this view effectively to boards, investors, and customers. Career Progression Software Engineer → Engineering Lead → VP/SVP Engineering → CTO. Some CTOs come from product management or architecture backgrounds. CTO tenures average 3–5 years, after which many move to board advisor, VC, or founder roles. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $200,000–$300,000 (CTO at mid-market companies), $300,000–$700,000 (large enterprise CTO total comp), $700,000–$5M+ (CTO at major tech companies with equity). Annual salary growth of 8–12% is projected, driven by sustained technology talent competition at senior levels. full_text: Quantum Cryptography / Post-Quantum Security Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview Post-Quantum Security Engineers develop and implement cryptographic systems that remain secure against quantum computer attacks, protecting data and communications in the post-quantum era. NIST's 2024 publication of the first post-quantum cryptography (PQC) standards (ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA) has triggered a major industry transition. Financial institutions, government agencies, and technology companies must migrate from RSA and ECC to quantum-resistant algorithms—a migration on the scale of the Y2K effort. Engineers who can lead this transition are in exceptional demand. What the Role Involves Work includes implementing and integrating NIST post-quantum algorithms (CRYSTALS-Kyber/ML-KEM, CRYSTALS-Dilithium/ML-DSA, SPHINCS+) into protocols, libraries, and products; conducting cryptographic inventories to identify quantum-vulnerable systems; designing hybrid classical-quantum cryptographic migration strategies; implementing TLS 1.3 extensions for PQC; advising on key management architecture evolution; and staying current on evolving NIST PQC standards and quantum computing progress. Organizations with long-lived sensitive data ("harvest now, decrypt later" threat) have urgent timelines for PQC adoption. Key Skills Required Deep cryptography knowledge (asymmetric cryptography, elliptic curves, lattice-based mathematics, hash-based signatures), software engineering (C, Rust, Go, or Python), and familiarity with cryptographic libraries (OpenSSL, BoringSSL, liboqs) and network security protocols (TLS, SSH, IPSec) are foundational. Knowledge of NIST PQC standards, formal cryptographic verification, and hardware security modules (HSMs) is essential for specialist roles. Soft skills: the ability to communicate complex cryptographic concepts to infrastructure teams and non-technical executives. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed Post-Quantum Security Engineers are deep specialists who are fascinated by the mathematics of cryptography and motivated by the existential importance of getting cryptography right. They are meticulous about correctness—cryptographic vulnerabilities are often subtle and catastrophic. They are also pragmatic engineers who can translate theoretical constructs into production implementations. Career Progression Security Engineer → Cryptography Engineer → Post-Quantum Security Engineer → Cryptography Architect → Head of Cryptography Engineering → VP of Security. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $130,000–$170,000 (engineer), $175,000–$230,000 (senior), $240,000–$350,000+ (architect/principal at financial services and government). Annual salary growth of 13–18% is projected, driven by the urgency of the PQC migration timeline. full_text: AI Infrastructure Engineer IT Workforce Profile | 10-Year Outlook (2025–2035) Role Category Technical Salary Growth (YoY) See Salary section below Remote Friendly High (majority of roles) Overview AI Infrastructure Engineers build and operate the large-scale computing infrastructure required to train and deploy AI models—from multi-GPU training clusters to high-throughput inference serving platforms. The exponential growth in AI model size and the proliferation of AI applications across enterprises has created an entirely new infrastructure specialty. Engineers who can design, manage, and optimize AI compute at scale are among the most valuable and scarcest professionals in the technology industry today, commanding compensation at the frontier of the engineering profession. What the Role Involves Work includes designing GPU/TPU training cluster architectures (NVIDIA A100/H100, TPU pods), managing distributed training frameworks (PyTorch Distributed, Horovod, DeepSpeed), optimizing training throughput (data parallelism, tensor parallelism, pipeline parallelism), building high-performance model serving infrastructure (Triton Inference Server, vLLM, TGI, Ray Serve), managing InfiniBand and high-speed networking for AI clusters, optimizing storage systems for training data pipelines (Lustre, GPFS, NVMe-over-Fabrics), managing CUDA environments, and implementing model quantization and serving optimization for cost-efficient inference. Key Skills Required Deep knowledge of GPU architecture (CUDA, cuDNN, NCCL), distributed systems, Kubernetes (with GPU device plugins), InfiniBand networking, and storage systems for AI is required. Experience with PyTorch, NVIDIA tooling (Nsight, DCGM), distributed training frameworks, and inference optimization libraries distinguishes expert practitioners. Linux systems administration at scale and familiarity with cloud AI platforms (AWS EC2 P/Trn instances, GCP TPUs, Azure NDv5 series) are expected. Soft skills: the ability to communicate infrastructure constraints to research teams and to find the optimal balance between infrastructure cost and training efficiency. What Kind of Person Tends to Succeed AI Infrastructure Engineers are passionate about the intersection of hardware and software performance—they derive deep satisfaction from squeezing more training throughput out of a GPU cluster. They are rigorous systems debuggers who can trace performance issues from the model level through framework internals down to hardware characteristics. They work closely with AI researchers and develop genuine appreciation for both the research goals and the operational realities of running AI at scale. Career Progression DevOps/HPC Engineer → AI Infrastructure Engineer → Senior AI Infrastructure Engineer → Staff AI Infrastructure Engineer → Principal Engineer or Head of AI Infrastructure → VP of AI Infrastructure. Many of the world's most valuable engineers work in this role at frontier AI labs. Salary Range & Annual Growth U.S. salaries: $130,000–$170,000 (mid-level), $175,000–$250,000 (senior), $260,000–$600,000+ (staff/principal at frontier AI labs including equity). This is among the highest-compensated engineering roles in the industry. Annual salary growth of 14–20% is projected through 2027, driven by the insatiable appetite of AI model development for compute.
